claims.notes: Confirmation hearing statement in the context of Senate vote on Kennedy's nomination as HHS Secretary.

claims.claim: COVID-19 vaccines "didn't prevent transmission" and "wouldn't prevent infection"—and "now everybody admits it."
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: FactCheck.org: Later data showed COVID-19 vaccination did reduce spread; the primary stated goal of the vaccines was prevention of severe disease, which they achieved; FDA's original EUA noted absence of data on transmission prevention. Verified via direct fetch.

claims.claim: "When my uncle was president, 2% of American kids had chronic disease. Today, 66% have chronic disease."
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: FactCheck.org: Kennedy's figure of 60–66% is an overestimate; CDC data from National Survey of Children's Health states more than 40% of school-aged children have at least one chronic condition; no comparative data exists for the early 1960s; broader definitions and better diagnosis explain much of any increase. Verified via direct fetch.

claims.claim: "No vaccine is safe and effective."
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: FactCheck.org: Kennedy made the statement "There's no vaccine that is, you know, safe and effective" in the July 2023 Lex Fridman interview; he claimed at his 2025 hearing he was interrupted before clarifying, but the review of the video shows he had finished the sentence. Verified via direct fetch. Date estimated YYYY-MM-01 per spec.

claims.claim: "Only one of those [childhood vaccines] have ever been tested against an inert placebo."
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: AFP Fact Check: Many childhood vaccines were originally tested in randomised clinical trials including placebo or comparison groups; the American Academy of Pediatrics confirms this. Verified via direct fetch.

claims.claim: "Did it [COVID-19 vaccine] save a million lives? Well there's no data to support that."
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: AFP Fact Check: A 2022 Lancet study estimated COVID-19 vaccines prevented 14.4 million deaths globally in their first year; CDC data showed vaccines prevented an estimated 235,000 U.S. deaths in the first year. Verified via direct fetch.

claims.claim: "Everybody can get the vaccine" [in response to senators alleging COVID-19 vaccine access was being restricted].
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: AFP Fact Check: FDA had issued guidance on 27 August 2025 limiting COVID-19 vaccine recommendations to those 65+ and those with qualifying health conditions. Verified via direct fetch.

claims.claim: Kennedy denied he had linked antidepressants (SSRIs) to school shootings, telling Sen. Tina Smith: "You're just making stuff up."
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: AP Fact Check: Kennedy had previously suggested SSRIs might contribute to violence; during the hearing he acknowledged HHS was studying whether SSRIs contribute to violence. Verified via direct fetch.

claims.claim: On the number of COVID-19 deaths: "I don't think anybody knows [how many died], because there was so much data chaos coming out of the CDC."
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: AP Fact Check: Approximately 1.2 million Americans died of COVID-19 according to both CDC and WHO; data is publicly available. Verified via direct fetch.

claims.claim: Kennedy claimed childhood vaccines were never tested against placebos: children need "6,992 vaccines" and "only one of those has been tested against a placebo."
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: AP Fact Check: CDC schedule recommends routine immunisation against 18 diseases; extensive safety and efficacy research exists including placebo-controlled studies; American Academy of Pediatrics affirms vaccine schedule safety. Verified via direct fetch.

claims.claim: "Two studies that show children who are circumcised early have double the rate of autism. It's highly likely because they're given Tylenol."
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: AFP Fact Check: A 2024 JAMA analysis found no link between Tylenol and autism; the studies Kennedy referenced did not support the claimed causal inference; research overwhelmingly shows autism causes are primarily genetic. Verified via direct fetch.

claims.claim: Kennedy testified he "probably did say" Lyme disease was a bioweapon created at Plum Island; on his 2024 podcast he said the disease is "highly likely to have been a military weapon."
claims.platform: Senate Finance Committee confirmation hearing / podcast
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: AFP Fact Check: Evidence shows Lyme disease has existed for tens of thousands of years; scientists at Plum Island do not and have not researched Lyme disease; researchers say it is a poor bioweapon candidate; a scientist who worked with the researcher Kennedy cited said comments were misinterpreted. Verified via direct fetch.

claims.claim: A Harvard doctor "cured schizophrenia using keto diet." / The ketogenic diet has been established as a cure for schizophrenia.
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: AFP Fact Check: The Harvard researcher referenced told AFP he "never used the word cure" in any of his presentations or work; the keto diet shows promise in early studies but has not been proven to cure schizophrenia. Verified via direct fetch.

claims.claim: mRNA vaccines "fail to protect effectively against upper respiratory infections like COVID and flu."
claims.platform: X post and HHS press release
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: Statement used to justify $500 million termination of federal mRNA vaccine research funding.

claims.claim: "One mutation, and the vaccine becomes ineffective."
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: FactCheck.org: Vaccine expert Dr Peter Hotez stated no "single mutation" has ever rendered an mRNA vaccine ineffective; mRNA vaccines target multiple sites on the virus. Verified via direct fetch. Also rated misleading by Full Fact (https://fullfact.org/health/robert-f-kennedy-mrna-vaccines/ 2025-08-19).

claims.claim: mRNA vaccines "paradoxically encourage new mutations and can actually prolong pandemics."
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: FactCheck.org: Mutations occur randomly; any vaccine or natural infection can drive viral evolution; this is not unique to mRNA vaccines. Verified via direct fetch.

claims.claim: Kennedy suggested Black people require a different vaccine schedule because they have a ""better"" immune system and ""only need half of the antigen that whites do."""
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: Reuters Fact Check: Studies Kennedy cited examined immune-response variation but did not support a claim that Black people need fewer antigens or that standard vaccine doses are harmful to them; a 2014 paper he cited was retracted for methodological issues. Verified via direct fetch.

claims.claim: The MMR vaccine contains "a lot of aborted fetal debris and DNA particles."
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: Reuters: The rubella component is grown using a fetal cell line from the 1960s but the final MMR vaccine does not contain fetal cells; trace DNA fragments are measured in billionths of a gram; specialists confirmed there is no "fetal debris" in the vaccine. Verified via direct fetch.

claims.claim: The mumps component of the MMR vaccine is ineffective and "the combination has never been safety tested."
claims.platform: Town hall event (Dr. Phil) and News Nation interview
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: Reuters: CDC states two doses of MMR offer approximately 86% protection against mumps; the combination vaccine has been extensively tested; while immunity can wane over time, vaccinated people experience milder illness. Verified via direct fetch.

claims.claim: "Fluoride is an industrial waste associated with arthritis, bone fractures, bone cancer, IQ loss, neurodevelopmental disorders, and thyroid disease."
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: Kennedy used this claim to call for removal of fluoride from U.S. water systems.

claims.claim: Kennedy said he was called a conspiracy theorist for claiming fluoride lowered IQ: "I was called a conspiracy theorist because I said fluoride lowered IQ. Last week JAMA published a meta review of 87 studies saying that there's a direct inverse correlation between IQ lost."
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: FactCheck.org: The JAMA Pediatrics paper was a meta-analysis of 74 studies (not 87); it has been described as controversial; many included studies had a high risk of bias; none were conducted in the U.S.; evidence at U.S. recommended levels is much weaker and inconsistent. Verified via direct fetch.

claims.claim: "Under my leadership, we have outperformed every other country in managing [the measles outbreak]."
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: AP Fact Check: Countries including Canada and Mexico had larger outbreaks than the U.S. in 2025; U.S. vaccination rates continued to decline; public health experts criticised Kennedy for hesitating to advocate vaccination and promoting alternative unverified treatments. Verified via search.

claims.claim: Kennedy claimed there are "no cuts to Medicaid": "We're increasing Medicaid by 47% over 10 years. Increasing by 47%—how is that a cut?"
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: AP Fact Check: Medicaid analysts said spending may rise due to demographics but the legislation enacted ~$1 trillion in funding reductions including new work requirements and eligibility changes; spending would have been higher without the cuts. Verified via search.

claims.claim: Dietary guidelines from the Biden administration "were influenced by the same commercial interests that placed Froot Loops at the apex of the food pyramid." The guidelines were 453 pages.
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: AP Fact Check: The food pyramid (replaced by MyPlate in 2011) did not feature specific brands; the current dietary guidelines are 164 pages not 453; the 2020 guidelines were published during Trump's first term not the Biden administration. Verified via search.

claims.claim: No childhood vaccines "have ever been tested in a safety study pre-licensing."
claims.platform: Fox & Friends / PBS NewsHour / CNN interviews (multiple)
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: Repeated claim throughout Kennedy's 2024 presidential campaign.

claims.claim: Vaccines cause autism; Kennedy stated he believes "autism does come from vaccines."
claims.platform: Fox News / multiple interviews and campaign events (2023)
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: Core claim throughout Kennedy's 2023–24 presidential campaign; PolitiFact named his campaign of conspiracy theories its 2023 Lie of the Year.

claims.claim: Implied or stated that HIV may not be the sole cause of AIDS; his 2021 book "The Real Anthony Fauci" approvingly quotes HIV deniers and he wrote that he "takes no position on the relationship between HIV and AIDS".
claims.platform: Book: ""The Real Anthony Fauci"" (published 2021); multiple interviews
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: FactCheck.org SciCheck and peer-reviewed commentary: Scientific consensus established since the 1980s that HIV causes AIDS; National Institutes of Health states HIV is the only consistent factor; Kennedy's claims echo the discredited Duesberg hypothesis. Date estimated YYYY-MM-01 per spec. Verified via Annenberg summary and search.

claims.claim: Ivermectin and hydroxychloroquine were discredited as COVID-19 treatments only so COVID-19 vaccines could be granted emergency use authorisation—a win for Big Pharma.
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: FactCheck.org SciCheck: Multiple large-scale trials showed ivermectin and hydroxychloroquine were ineffective against COVID-19; EUA criteria for vaccines are separate from treatment effectiveness evaluations. Date estimated YYYY-MM-01 per spec. Verified via Annenberg summary.

claims.claim: Kennedy suggested vaccines caused the 1918 Spanish flu pandemic, claiming he had "strong evidence" of this at least twice during his 2024 presidential campaign.
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: AFP Fact Check roundup of Kennedy false and misleading vaccine claims: The 1918 influenza pandemic was caused by a naturally occurring H1N1 virus; influenza vaccines did not exist until the 1930s; claim originates from a debunked meningitis-vaccine conspiracy. Snopes has also covered this claim (widely cited). Date estimated YYYY-MM-01 per spec.

claims.claim: "Robert F. Kennedy Jr. did not win a Supreme Court case that ruled the COVID-19 vaccine causes irreparable damage." [Claim attributed to Kennedy to lend false credibility to the hoax.]
claims.platform: Social media posts (viral claim mis-attributed to Kennedy)
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: Lead Stories: No such Supreme Court case exists in the court's docket; Kennedy himself has described similar claims as misinformation. Date estimated YYYY-MM-01 per spec. Verified via search result.

claims.claim: Atrazine (herbicide) exposure "probably" contributes to gender dysphoria and sexual dysphoria in children.
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: PolitiFact 2023 Lie of the Year compilation: Kennedy linked environmental chemicals to gender dysphoria in children without credible evidence; his spokesman later said he was calling for investigation, but Kennedy repeatedly made causal-sounding statements. Date estimated YYYY-MM-01 per spec. Verified via search.

claims.claim: SSRIs (antidepressants) are causally linked to mass shootings: there is "tremendous circumstantial evidence" linking psychiatric drugs to school shootings.
claims.platform: Livestream with Elon Musk; multiple public statements
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: FactCheck.org and PolitiFact: Psychiatry experts state there is no causal relationship between antidepressants and school shootings; data on school shooters shows most had no history of psychiatric medication; ~13% of U.S. adults use antidepressants without elevated violence rates. Date estimated YYYY-MM-01 per spec. Also corroborated by AP Fact Check (2025-09-04).

claims.claim: Supplementary data from a large Danish study on aluminium-containing vaccines "shows calamitous evidence of harm" and the study should be retracted.
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: FactCheck.org: The Danish study followed 1.2 million children over 24 years and found no link between aluminium in vaccines and any of 50 chronic conditions including autism; Kennedy cherry-picked a subgroup of 51 cases from supplementary data; the journal's editor found no grounds for retraction. URL verified via FactCheck.org Substack post referencing the article.

claims.claim: Only one childhood vaccine has been tested in a randomised placebo-controlled trial; vaccines recommended for U.S. children have not been tested against inert placebos.
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: AFP Fact Check: Many childhood vaccines were originally tested in randomised clinical trials with placebo or comparison groups, per the American Academy of Pediatrics. Verified via direct fetch.

claims.claim: SSRIs are "harder to give up than heroin" and "school shootings became prevalent only after the introduction of Prozac."
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: PolitiFact: No studies have established causation between SSRIs and mass violence; a majority of school shooters had no history of taking these medications; health organisations, psychiatrists, and researchers describe the statements as riddled with falsehoods. Date estimated YYYY-MM-01 per spec. Verified via search.

claims.claim: Measles vaccine "wanes about 4.5% per year"; implied that measles vaccination provides declining protection each year.
claims.impact: fact-checked
claims.notes: Kennedy told Fox News Hannity that the MMR vaccine "wanes" and later told the Senate HELP Committee it was "all true" the vaccine wanes quickly. PolitiFact (May 2025) rated this False: two-dose MMR is 97% effective and provides lifelong protection; infectious disease experts say immunity does not wane in any clinically meaningful way; if it did, thousands of vaccinated people in the 2025 Texas outbreak would have been infected.

claims.claim: We're not cutting science. We're not cutting research.
claims.notes: HHS terminated thousands of federal grants affecting clinical trials; about 2,500 grants remained terminated or frozen as of 19 Nov 2025, directly contradicting Kennedy's claim.

claims.claim: There's two ways of calculating percentage decreases. If you have a $600 drug and you reduce it to $10, that's a 600% reduction.
claims.notes: Only one standard method exists for calculating percentage decreases. A $590 reduction from $600 equals a 98.3% decrease; any decrease from a positive number cannot mathematically exceed 100%.

claims.claim: It's going to be the biggest infusion of federal dollars into rural health care in American history.
claims.notes: The $50B Rural Health Transformation Program is more than offset by Medicaid cuts projected to cost rural areas at least $137B over a decade per KFF analysis; new fund covers only ~37% of those losses and does not guarantee spending goes to rural healthcare.

claims.claim: It's all true that the measles vaccine wanes quickly, was never fully safety-tested, and contains fetal debris.
claims.notes: Two-dose MMR is ~97% effective and provides lifelong protection. MMR underwent safety testing before approval. Vaccine contains trace fragmented DNA—not intact fetal cells—in quantities far below any biological significance.

claims.claim: We don't know what was killing them [about the 2019 Samoa measles deaths]; tissue samples showed most of those people did not have measles.
claims.notes: Samoa Director-General of Health Dr. Alec Ekeroma called Kennedy's claims 'a total fabrication.' Only one autopsy was conducted and no post-mortem tissue was sent overseas; 83 children died from confirmed measles.

claims.claim: There are adverse events from the vaccine, it does cause deaths every year, it causes all the illnesses that measles itself causes, encephalitis and blindness.
claims.notes: No reliable evidence the MMR vaccine causes deaths annually; adverse event reports do not establish causation. Measles infection is far more likely than the vaccine to cause encephalitis, blindness, or death. Creates a false equivalency between vaccine risk and disease risk.

claims.claim: The death from measles historically was between 1 in 1,200 and 1 in 10,000 so it does not have a high infection fatality rate and the people who tended to die were people who had comorbidities and they were malnourished.
claims.notes: About 1 in 1,000 measles cases result in death even with treatment and about 3 in 10 children develop complications; understates disease severity and misdirects focus from preventability through vaccination.

claims.claim: The child that died didn't die from the measles but it was a bacterial infection.
claims.notes: The 8-year-old Texas child died from 'measles pulmonary failure'—a direct complication of measles, not a separate bacterial infection.

claims.claim: Two Texas doctors have treated and healed around 300 measles-infected Mennonites using aerosolized budesonide and clarithromycin.
claims.notes: No cure exists for measles. The American Academy of Pediatrics described use of budesonide and clarithromycin for measles as 'risky and unproven.' No peer-reviewed evidence supports this treatment claim.

claims.claim: 70% of Americans are either obese or overweight compared to just 3% during [JFK's] administration.
claims.notes: CDC data shows ~13% of Americans were obese in 1960–62—not 3%. The current ~74% overweight figure combines overweight and obese categories, which Kennedy conflates with obesity alone.

claims.claim: Twenty years ago, there was no diabetes in China. Today, 50% of the population is diabetic.
claims.notes: Latest data shows China's diabetes prevalence at approximately 12.4%, not 50%. Diabetes was not non-existent in China 20 years ago; rates have grown but Kennedy's figures are wildly overstated.

claims.claim: We have demonstrated that [food dyes] directly impact academic performance, school violence, as well as mental and physical health.
claims.notes: FDA website states most children have no adverse effects from colour additives. Limited evidence links some dyes to hyperactivity in sensitive children but no causal proof exists for academic performance or school violence.

claims.claim: We know [autism] is an environmental exposure and we know it's not genetic because genes don't cause epidemics.
claims.notes: Scientific consensus identifies a combination of genetic and environmental factors in autism aetiology; the claim that genetics cannot contribute to prevalence increases is not supported by evidence.

claims.claim: There's two studies that show children who are circumcised early have double the rate of autism, and it's highly likely because they're given Tylenol.
claims.notes: Neither of the cited Danish studies measured acetaminophen use; they showed correlation only and acknowledged inability to establish causation. No peer-reviewed study establishes that Tylenol causes autism.

claims.claim: The United States literally has the sickest population in the world.
claims.platform: TV interview (Fox News with Laura Ingraham)
claims.notes: FactCheck.org found this to be an exaggeration; while the U.S. has significant chronic disease burden, multiple metrics do not support the claim that it is the world's sickest population.

claims.claim: Americans buy 70% of the pharmaceutical drugs on Earth.
claims.platform: TV interview (Fox News with Laura Ingraham)
claims.notes: FactCheck.org found this claim to be wrong; the U.S. accounts for a large share of pharmaceutical spending but not 70% of all drugs consumed globally.

claims.claim: Replacing seed oils used to cook Big Macs with beef tallow would make them good for people.
claims.platform: TV interview (Fox News with Laura Ingraham)
claims.notes: No scientific evidence supports the claim that beef-tallow Big Macs would be broadly healthy; nutrition experts note saturated fat content and other ingredients are still health concerns.

claims.claim: The NIH told doctors and patients not to report injuries after taking the abortion drug.
claims.platform: TV interview (Fox News with Laura Ingraham)
claims.notes: FactCheck.org found Kennedy incorrectly claimed and mischaracterised NIH guidance; no such instruction to withhold adverse-event reporting was issued.

claims.claim: Almost all members of HHS advisory committees have severe conflicts of interest.
claims.platform: TV interview (Fox News with Laura Ingraham)
claims.notes: Federal ethics rules require advisory committee members to disclose and recuse on conflicts; a JAMA study later found only 6.2% of CDC ACIP members had conflicts, contradicting Kennedy's broad claim.

claims.claim: Vaccines are the one medical product that is exempt from prelicensing safety studies.
claims.platform: TV interview (Fox News with Laura Ingraham)
claims.notes: FactCheck.org found the claim false and misleading; vaccines must pass rigorous clinical trials, including Phase I-III safety studies, before FDA licensure.

claims.claim: SSRIs might be contributing to violence in school shootings; SSRIs have black box warnings for homicidal ideation.
claims.notes: Experts say there is no direct evidence linking SSRIs to mass shootings. SSRIs do carry a black box warning for suicidal thoughts in young people—not homicidal ideation—so Kennedy's specific claim about the warning is false.

claims.claim: Today, on the CDC's website right now, they list the 10 top advances—the 10 greatest advances in medical science. And one of them is abortion.
claims.notes: CDC's Ten Great Public Health Achievements list does not mention abortion. Jake Tapper searched the CDC site live and found zero references to abortion as an achievement; HHS later acknowledged the word 'abortion' appeared only in the context of unintended pregnancies under 'family planning.'

claims.claim: A CDC study in 1999 revealed autism risk skyrocketed by 1,135% among children vaccinated against Hepatitis B; the CDC then watered down the study to cover its conclusion.
claims.notes: The 1999 data were a conference abstract, not a peer-reviewed study. A full 2003 peer-reviewed study by the same researchers found no significant autism risk increase. CDC did not suppress the results—the 1999 abstract and 2000 Simpsonwood conference proceedings were publicly available. One TikTok video spreading the claim received 240,000+ views.

claims.claim: RFK and Andy Wakefield were RIGHT—vaccines cause autism (promoting a flawed analysis of Florida Medicaid data as proof).
claims.notes: The Medicaid data analysis is methodologically flawed; it used billing codes to determine vaccination status, missing children vaccinated through the Vaccines for Children programme. No large-scale rigorous study has found a vaccine–autism link. Post received 12,000+ likes and 5,000+ retweets on X. | Science Feedback topic-titled review covering the Florida-Medicaid vaccine-autism claim RFK Jr amplified. URL verified live (HTTP 200).

claims.claim: The government is covering up vaccine deaths using the WHO AEFI causality assessment protocol to classify post-vaccination deaths as coincidental.
claims.platform: Article published on Children's Health Defense website
claims.notes: WHO AEFI protocol does allow post-licensure deaths to be investigated for vaccine causation. The US uses its own VAERS system, not WHO AEFI. Science Feedback found multiple factual errors; NewsPunch subsequently issued a correction. Article shared 200,000+ times on Facebook.

claims.claim: One vaccine injury occurs for every 39 vaccinations administered.
claims.platform: Article published on Children's Health Defense website
claims.notes: The 1-in-39 figure is drawn from VAERS, which records adverse events after vaccination but cannot establish causation. Kennedy commits a post hoc fallacy; scientific literature confirms childhood vaccine schedules are safe. Facebook later removed the post.

claims.claim: Leucovorin is an exciting therapy that may benefit large numbers of children who suffer from autism.
claims.notes: FDA approval of leucovorin covers cerebral folate deficiency—a specific neurological condition—not autism broadly. Experts note it may help a subset of children but there is no evidence it benefits large numbers of autistic children as Kennedy implied.

claims.claim: mRNA vaccines cause serious harm, including death, especially among young people.
claims.notes: CDC concluded there is no increased risk of death associated with COVID-19 mRNA vaccines. Kennedy agreed with this claim at the hearing; experts say the claim is not supported by evidence and contradicts large-scale safety surveillance data.

claims.claim: A new Danish study on Tylenol and autism is garbage, industry-generated and fraudulent and should be retracted.
claims.notes: FactCheck.org found no evidence of fraud or industry involvement. The criticism Kennedy cited—a methodological limitation—was already acknowledged by the study's authors and does not constitute legitimate grounds for retraction according to scientists.

claims.claim: There is no clinical data supporting COVID-19 vaccine use in children.
claims.notes: AFP found the claim false; randomised controlled trials and extensive real-world evidence demonstrate safety and efficacy of COVID-19 vaccines in children. Senate minority staff report identified this as one of several false claims at the hearing.

claims.claim: A doctor at Harvard has cured schizophrenia using keto diets.
claims.notes: Harvard psychiatrist Dr. Christopher Palmer told Science Feedback he 'never used the word cure in any of my presentations or any of my work.' Evidence for keto diet in schizophrenia comes from small case reports lacking control groups; large randomised controlled trials are still underway. Science Feedback verdict: Unsupported.

claims.claim: Israel has allowed over 40,000 aid trucks into Gaza — half a million tons of food, more than 3,000 calories for every man, woman and child. If there are Palestinians in Gaza who aren't getting enough food, it's not because Israel is blocking it. It's because Hamas is stealing it.
claims.impact: Claim made to joint session of US Congress; circulated globally. AP and PBS reporting documented the role of IDF checkpoint restrictions and closures — not only Hamas looting — in limiting aid distribution. A US envoy stated Israel had provided no evidence Hamas was systematically stealing UN aid. Claim contributed to international debate over Israel's compliance with US aid conditions tied to weapons transfers.
claims.notes: Verified via direct fetch of AP article (published 2024-07-24). AP assessment: false/lacking key context. Corroborating PBS NewsHour fact-check (https://www.pbs.org/newshour/politics/fact-checking-israeli-prime-minister-netanyahus-claims-in-his-address-to-congress). REVIEW: claim is closely adjacent to ICC prosecutor allegations regarding use of starvation as a weapon of war (active ICC proceedings, Nov 2024 arrest warrant issued); human editor should verify before publication.

claims.claim: Iran is funding the anti-Israel protests [outside the US Capitol]. When the tyrants of Tehran who hang gays from cranes and murder women for not covering their hair are praising, promoting and funding you, you have officially become Iran's useful idiots.
claims.notes: Verified via direct fetch of AP article. AP assessment: Netanyahu provided no evidence that Iran is financially supporting US campus/street protesters. US Director of National Intelligence Avril Haines confirmed Iranian influence efforts via social media, but explicitly stated US protesters were acting in good faith and her intelligence did not indicate otherwise. Netanyahu's blanket claim of Iranian funding conflated influence operations with mass protest participation. Corroborating PBS NewsHour fact-check (https://www.pbs.org/newshour/politics/fact-checking-israeli-prime-minister-netanyahus-claims-in-his-address-to-congress).

claims.claim: Despite all the lies you've heard, the war in Gaza has one of the lowest ratios of combatants to noncombatants, casualties in the history of urban warfare. And you want to know where it's lowest in Gaza? It's lowest in Rafah.
claims.verdict: Cannot be verified; lacks important context
claims.impact: Claim made to US Congress and repeated at September 2024 press conference (Netanyahu stated '1-to-1' ratio). AP and independent analysts noted the claim is impossible to verify due to absence of objective data; available evidence contradicts it. IDF's own leaked internal report (August 2025) reportedly indicated over 80% of those killed were civilians.
claims.notes: Verified via direct fetch of AP article. AP assessment: claim lacks important context and is impossible to verify; neither Netanyahu's own casualty estimates nor Hamas Ministry of Health data distinguishes combatants from civilians. Independent academic analysis (Action on Armed Violence, Aug 2024) concluded the claim is not supported. REVIEW: directly intersects with ICC prosecutor allegations of deliberate targeting of civilians (active ICC arrest warrant issued Nov 2024); human editor must vet before publication.

claims.claim: There is no policy of starvation in Gaza. And there is no starvation in Gaza.
claims.platform: TV interview / public remarks (Daystar evangelical network event Jerusalem)
claims.impact: Claim made on same day Israel began 10-hour daily pauses in fighting to allow limited aid in; widely cited internationally as contradicting documented reality. US President Trump publicly disagreed with Netanyahu on this specific point two days later.
claims.notes: Verified via direct fetch of PolitiFact article (published 2025-07-29, claim date 2025-07-27). PolitiFact rating: Pants on Fire. Evidence cited by PolitiFact: WHO reported 63 malnutrition deaths in July 2025 alone (24 children under 5); UN World Food Program said one-third of Gaza population not eating for days-long stretches; 100+ humanitarian organisations declared mass starvation on July 23 2025; Médecins Sans Frontières recorded all-time high malnutrition levels. REVIEW: starvation-as-weapon claim is closely adjacent to active ICC proceedings (arrest warrant issued Nov 2024 covering crimes against humanity in Gaza); human editor should vet before publication.

claims.claim: The International Criminal Court prosecutor accuses Israel of deliberately targeting civilians. What is he talking about? The Israeli military has disseminated millions of flyers, sent countless text messages, and made hundreds of thousands of phone calls to evacuate Palestinian civilians from danger. Meanwhile, Hamas does all it can to endanger Palestinian civilians, launching rockets from schools, hospitals, and mosques.
claims.verdict: THE FACTS section contradicts claim (no explicit AP label)
claims.notes: AP found: deadly Israeli airstrikes on residential areas affect multiple family members daily; footage shows soldiers killing Palestinians who were surrendering or waving white flags; for most of Israel's 37,000+ airstrikes it is impossible to verify Hamas targets were present; Israeli evacuation orders sent over 80% of Gaza's population fleeing, yet Israeli strikes repeatedly hit designated 'humanitarian zones.' AP's THE FACTS section directly contradicts Netanyahu's implicit claim that the ICC was wrong to raise concerns about civilian targeting. LEGAL REVIEW NEEDED (ICC proceedings active — Netanyahu subject to ICC arrest warrant application).

claims.claim: Improvements in Iran's nuclear program in the past year would allow it to push past the barrier of 20 percent enriched uranium to reach 90 percent enrichment within 'weeks at most.'
claims.verdict: THE FACTS section contradicts claim (no explicit AP label)
claims.notes: AP (Brian Murphy, bylined AP, syndicated at KSL) found significant expert debate over the timetable: Iran's 20%-enriched stockpile was then ~200 kg, short of the ~250 kg threshold experts estimated for one bomb. New-generation centrifuges were installed but not yet running. AP noted 'many experts say it could be several months or longer' — not weeks as Netanyahu claimed.

claims.claim: Iran's planned heavy water reactor in the city of Arak has 'no connection with energy for peace, but only for nuclear weapons.'
claims.verdict: THE FACTS section contradicts claim (no explicit AP label)
claims.notes: AP found that the Arak reactor could produce plutonium as a byproduct potentially usable in weapons, but Iran did not yet possess the special extraction and enrichment process required to weaponise it. Iran stated the reactor's primary purpose was producing medical isotopes for cancer treatment, and had agreed to 24-hour IAEA video surveillance. AP reported the claim overstated the weapons-only connection.

claims.claim: I turn to the public out of concern that we will not be able to form the government because of the enormous support to them from foreign associations which are giving them tens of millions of dollars.
claims.notes: Washington Post Fact Checker (Glenn Kessler) awarded Two Pinocchios, finding that Netanyahu exaggerated: much of the funding in question came from European governments and Jewish citizens of other countries and in many cases only indirectly played a role in the election campaign. Kessler noted Netanyahu 'is believed to have politically benefited from the efforts of American billionaire Sheldon Adelson' as a counter-example. Two Pinocchios = 'significant omissions and/or exaggerations; a politician can create a false, misleading impression.'

claims.claim: [Palestinian Authority President] Abbas pays about $350 million a year to terrorists and their families, each year. That's about a little less than 10 percent of the total Palestinian budget.
claims.notes: Washington Post Fact Checker (Glenn Kessler) awarded Two Pinocchios. After reviewing PA budget documents and data provided by the PLO, Kessler found it is 'hard to use a broad brush and claim all $350 million goes to reward terrorism' and that 'the exact number is classified in part because U.S. officials have little confidence in the estimates.' Kessler's own calculation placed the figure significantly lower than $350 million. The $350 million figure was found to be a significant exaggeration. Glenn Kessler confirmed this rating in a 2025 CJR interview: 'I ended up giving two Pinocchios to Benjamin Netanyahu, which resulted in an official Israeli complaint to the State Department.'

claims.claim: Muslims have the first right to the country's resources — that is what Manmohan Singh's government said. This means they will gather all the wealth and distribute it among those who have more children, among infiltrators.
claims.notes: Modi cited Manmohan Singh's 2006 NDC speech, stripping it of context; full PMO transcript shows Singh referred to priority welfare for SC/ST/OBC/minorities collectively, not Muslims exclusively receiving national wealth. Alt News found the claim "FALSE at two levels." (translated from Hindi)

claims.claim: The Congress manifesto says it will survey and seize the gold of our mothers and sisters — their mangalsutras — and redistribute it to Muslims.
claims.impact: High — triggered Election Commission complaints by Congress
claims.notes: The Congress 2024 manifesto contains no reference to confiscating gold jewellery or any wealth redistribution to Muslims. Alt News: "Neither does the manifesto talk about it." LEGAL REVIEW NEEDED: communal framing of specific religious group.

claims.claim: The Congress manifesto looks more like a manifesto of the Muslim League.
claims.impact: High — repeated across multiple states
claims.notes: The word 'Muslim' does not appear in the Congress 2024 manifesto. Quint WebQoof and Alt News both debunked this characterisation. LEGAL REVIEW NEEDED.

claims.claim: None of the parties in the INDIA alliance are contesting for a majority — 272 seats — in the upcoming elections.
claims.notes: As on 18 April 2024 Congress was contesting 278 seats; by 2 May it had 305 candidates. WebQoof: claim "debunked." Congress fielded well above the 272-seat threshold.

claims.claim: Since the elections have been declared Rahul Gandhi has stopped criticising Ambani and Adani.
claims.notes: WebQoof reviewed Gandhi's speeches from 20 April onwards and found "multiple mentions of Gautam Adani and Mukesh Ambani." Verdict: "PM Modi's claim ... is false."

claims.claim: I have never used the words Hindu or Muslim in my election addresses; my speeches have no communal undertones.
claims.notes: WebQoof reviewed all Modi speeches in the week prior to the interview and found he mentioned Hindus or Muslims five times. Alt News separately confirmed. Verdict: "The Prime Minister's claim is false."

claims.claim: I have never indulged in Hindu-Muslim rhetoric in my election speeches.
claims.notes: Alt News documented instances of communal framing in speeches in Chatra, Tonk-Sawai Madhopur, Khargone, Dumka (2019), and Nanded. Verdict: "unfounded and false." LEGAL REVIEW NEEDED: communal incitement context.

claims.claim: Today, every village in India is open defecation free.
claims.notes: NFHS-5 (2019-21) showed 17% of households still practised open defecation; 5.6% of rural households had no toilet per NARSS. Verdict: the 100% ODF claim is "inaccurate."

claims.claim: More than 99% of the Indian population has a gas connection for clean cooking.
claims.notes: Both government (NFHS-5) and international sources showed well below 99% access to clean cooking fuel. Alt News: "both government sources, as well as international sources, do not indicate" the claimed figure.

claims.claim: Electricity has reached every village in the country.
claims.notes: The government's own 'electrified village' definition (since 1997) requires only 10% of households to have power, not 100%. Alt News: "the claim that India has achieved 100% electricity in all its villages is based on an insincere definition."

claims.claim: UP is the state that conducts the highest testing and vaccination in the country.
claims.notes: On a per-lakh basis UP had 3,516 fully vaccinated per lakh, the second-lowest in India against a national average of 7,575. Alt News: the claim is "false or misleading."

claims.claim: Until four years ago Uttar Pradesh had only a dozen medical colleges. Now they have increased by 4 times.
claims.notes: MoHFW data showed UP had 38 medical colleges in 2016 and 57 in 2021 — an increase of 50%, not 400%. A fourfold increase would require 152 colleges. Alt News: "not even close to a four-fold increase."

claims.claim: I first used a digital camera, probably in 1987 or 1988, and I transmitted a colour photo of Advani to Delhi by email the same day.
claims.notes: The first commercial digital camera (Kodak DCS 100) was sold in 1991; public internet in India launched in 1995. AFWA: Modi's claim "cannot be substantiated with credible facts."

claims.claim: The previous UPA government constructed only 25 lakh houses in their last 4 years, whereas the BJP government built 1.25 crore houses.
claims.notes: Government's own Rajya Sabha responses showed UPA built 89.65 lakh rural houses in its last four years (2010-14) — 3.5 times Modi's stated figure. Factly: "this claim is FALSE."

claims.claim: NRC was never discussed by my government since I took over as PM in 2014.
claims.notes: Home Minister Amit Shah had stated on record in Parliament and multiple public forums that a nationwide NRC would be implemented. Factly: "the PM's statement that NRC was never discussed by his government is misleading."

claims.claim: There are no detention centres in India.
claims.notes: Government's own parliamentary answers confirmed the existence of detention centres and plans to build new ones. Factly: "the claim of PM Narendra Modi is False."

claims.claim: India stopped Russia-Ukraine war temporarily for 6-8 hours to evacuate Indians.
claims.notes: India's own Ministry of External Affairs spokesperson Arindam Bagchi called the claim "absolutely inaccurate" in a press conference. Surfaced March 2022; amplified by BJP official handles. Checker: Alt News 2022 roundup documents repeated official denial.

claims.claim: The Congress manifesto says the government will seize and redistribute private property — if you have two houses, one will be taken away.
claims.impact: High — Election Commission issued notices to BJP
claims.notes: The Congress manifesto contains no reference to seizing private property. Quint: the claim is "false." Scroll.in (28 Apr 2024) corroborates; Congress filed EC complaint.

claims.claim: The Congress will impose inheritance tax; they will survey your wealth and tax what you pass to your children — the Congress even said this in 2011.
claims.impact: High
claims.notes: The Congress 2024 manifesto contains no reference to inheritance tax. P Chidambaram mooted the idea personally in 2011 but it was never Congress policy. The 55% tax referenced was from the US (Sam Pitroda comment). Quint: "false."

claims.claim: The Congress will steal OBC reservations and give them to Muslims on the basis of religion.
claims.impact: High
claims.notes: The Congress 2024 manifesto makes no reference to religion-based reservations; the OBC quota is untouched. Quint: claim is "false." LEGAL REVIEW NEEDED: communal framing.

claims.claim: India is the fastest-growing large economy in the world and has been so consistently.
claims.notes: BJP infographic claimed GDP growth forecast of 7.4% for FY2018-19; second advance estimate revised down to 7%. Factly: "Hence the claim is FALSE." India was not always fastest-growing across all measured years.

claims.claim: India has moved from the 11th to the 4th largest economy during my tenure.
claims.notes: IMF World Economic Outlook (April 2025) showed India at #5 (behind Japan) with only estimates — not confirmed data — showing possible overtake in FY26. Alt News: claim citing IMF data "misleading." India currently remains the fifth-largest.

claims.claim: The Congress wanted to defeat President Droupadi Murmu in the presidential election because her skin colour is dark.
claims.impact: High — dragged sitting President into election campaign
claims.notes: No factual basis cited by Modi; Quint opinion and Alt News both document this as a "blatant lie" with no evidentiary basis. LEGAL REVIEW NEEDED: defamatory framing involving the office of President.

claims.claim: The Congress in Karnataka included all Muslim communities in the OBC quota by a single notification, snatching OBC reservations for Muslims.
claims.notes: The Karnataka OBC sub-category for Muslims dates to the 1921 Mysore Maharaja-era policy, formalised by R Nagana Gowda Commission in 1962 — not a single-notification change by the current Congress government. Quint: misleading claim debunked.

claims.claim: India is open defecation free — 110 million toilets have been built and 600 million people given toilet access in 60 months.
claims.notes: NFHS-5 and NARSS data show open defecation continued in 17-26% of households. Quint: claim that all Indian villages are ODF is "inaccurate." (date_claimed reflects 2 Oct 2019 formal ODF declaration; fact-check published 2022)

claims.claim: If Congress comes to power they will snatch away the gold of our mothers and sisters through a survey of wealth.
claims.impact: High
claims.notes: The Congress manifesto contains no reference to surveying or seizing jewellery or any form of personal gold. Quint: "false claims." LEGAL REVIEW NEEDED: communal framing targeting Hindu women.

claims.claim: The Congress party says the celebration of Ram Navami and going to temples is anti-national; they are hatching a conspiracy to erase Hindu faith.
claims.notes: No Congress leader or document states Ram Navami or temple-going is anti-national. Alt News documented this claim as part of documented 2024 rally disinformation pattern. Quint opinion characterises it as a "blatant lie."

claims.claim: If there is a box of bajra grain in your home with more than you need, the Congress X-ray will seize it and redistribute it.
claims.notes: Rahul Gandhi's X-ray/survey comment referred to a socio-economic and caste census to measure inequality — no reference to seizing food or domestic goods. Alt News: "FALSE."

claims.claim: India has administered 100 crore doses to its citizens for free, without taking any money.
claims.notes: Prior to 21 June 2021 vaccines were not free for all age groups; the government's own data showed 6–7% of doses were administered in paid private centres. Quint: "it is misleading and inaccurate to claim that all the vaccinations which were given were free of cost."

claims.claim: The government has given five million LPG connections under the Ujjwala Yojana scheme.
claims.platform: Rally speech (Red Fort Independence Day address)
claims.notes: India Today checked: only applications cleared numbered 3.5 million; connections actually issued were 1.7 million — well below the five million claimed. India Today found the 5 million figure was of applications cleared, not connections issued.

claims.claim: Solar energy in India grew by 116 percent over the past year.
claims.platform: Rally speech (Red Fort Independence Day address)
claims.notes: India Today checked: actual solar energy growth between June 2015 and June 2016 was 95% (4 GW to 7.8 GW), not 116% as claimed. Same Independence Day speech fact-check article.

claims.claim: Uddhav Thackeray referred to Mughal emperor Aurangzeb as his brother in a public speech
claims.notes: BJP MLA Maharashtra Nitesh Rane shared a clipped video falsely claiming Thackeray called Aurangzeb his brother. Alt News found the video was edited and taken out of context; Thackeray was referencing a historical/cultural speech topic.

claims.claim: A video shows a successful trial run of a train on the Chenab railway bridge in Jammu and Kashmir on 21 March
claims.notes: BJP spokesperson RP Singh shared a viral video claimed to show the Chenab bridge trial run. Alt News identified the bridge as the Beipanjiang Railway Bridge (Shuibai) in China. The Chenab bridge was still under construction at the time.

claims.claim: This photo shows Rahul Gandhi meeting the producer of the BBC documentary on PM Modi six months before it was released
claims.notes: Former Deputy CM of J&K Kavinder Gupta tweeted the photo with caption 'Rahul with BBC documentary producer six months back. So it seems producer had planned the documentary six months back and financed by……..?' Alt News found the person in the photo is UK Conservative MP Bob Blackman, not a BBC producer.

claims.claim: Rahul Gandhi met the BBC documentary producer six months before the film aired showing the documentary was pre-planned and financially supported
claims.notes: Kavinder Gupta made an implied conspiracy theory alongside the misidentified photo. The person in the photo is UK Conservative MP Bob Blackman who hosted Rahul Gandhi at an Indian diaspora event in the UK. No BBC producer connection established.

claims.claim: Between 2001 and 2011 the Hindu population grew 16.8% while the Muslim population grew 24.6% and this disparity is due to infiltration of Muslims from abroad
claims.impact: Posted ahead of Bihar West Bengal and Assam elections to communally mobilise Hindu voters.
claims.notes: Office of Home Minister Amit Shah (@AmitShahOffice) shared Census data attributing Muslim population growth to 'infiltration'. Alt News found both communities' decadal growth rates are falling with Muslims' declining faster; no evidence supports the 'infiltration' explanation. LEGAL REVIEW NEEDED — communal-violence-attribution context.

claims.claim: The West Bengal government does not grant government holidays during Durga Puja while granting holidays during Ramzan
claims.impact: Made at Lok Sabha 2024 election rally in Serampore West Bengal.
claims.notes: Amit Shah at Serampore rally claimed Bengal discriminates by giving Ramzan but not Durga Puja holidays. Alt News verified the official 2024 West Bengal holiday list which contains multiple Durga Puja holidays including Maha Saptami Maha Ashtami and Dasami. Claim is blatantly false.

claims.claim: In West Bengal only imams and mullahs receive government salaries; Hindu priests receive nothing
claims.impact: Made at Lok Sabha 2024 election rally in Serampore West Bengal.
claims.notes: At the same Serampore rally Amit Shah claimed only Islamic clergy get Bengal government salaries. Alt News verified that Hindu priests also receive monthly stipends from the Bengal government (announced 2013) though the amount differs.

claims.claim: PM Modi stopped the Russia–Ukraine war for 6 hours so that Indian students could be safely evacuated
claims.impact: Made at election rally; false claim about India's global influence to appeal to nationalist sentiment.
claims.notes: Amit Shah repeated at an election rally the false claim that Modi's phone call halted the Russia-Ukraine war. India's MEA spokesperson had categorically called the claim 'absolutely inaccurate' in a special briefing on 3 March 2022.

claims.claim: PM Modi's calls to Russian and Ukrainian presidents halted the war so Indian students could evacuate safely
claims.notes: Amit Shah made this claim at an election rally in November 2022. India's MEA had said on 3 March 2022: 'Extrapolating that to say that somebody is holding up bombing is absolutely inaccurate.' BJP leaders including Shah and Nadda repeated the MEA-denied claim eight months later.

claims.claim: The Mamata Banerjee government does not grant Durga Puja holidays but does grant Ramzan holidays — this is communal discrimination
claims.impact: Made during Lok Sabha 2024 election campaign in West Bengal.
claims.notes: Second documented instance of same false claim by Amit Shah (same Serampore rally; same fact-check article). Alt News retrieved the official West Bengal Finance Department holiday notice for 2024 showing Durga Puja holidays on 7-15 October.

claims.claim: No other Prime Minister in the history of India has been as great as Modi Ji. He stopped the Russia–Ukraine war to evacuate 22500 students from there back to India
claims.notes: BJP President JP Nadda posted this claim on Twitter eight months after the MEA had denied any war pause. Alt News documented Nadda's tweet specifically alongside Amit Shah and other BJP leaders repeating the same claim debunked by India's own government.

claims.claim: PM Modi is the only PM in history who stopped a war to bring students home safely
claims.notes: BJP President JP Nadda's tweet explicitly says Modi 'stopped Russia-Ukraine War to evacuate 22,500 students'. The claim was already denied by MEA in March 2022. Article documents this as a BJP-wide coordinated false narrative.

claims.claim: Rahul Gandhi is using a body double in the Bharat Jodo Nyay Yatra; the person in the bus window is not Gandhi
claims.notes: Assam CM Himanta Biswa Sarma held a press conference on 25 January 2024 asserting the 'body double' claim with certainty. Alt News identified the person in the bus as Congress worker Rakesh Kushwaha via his Facebook page showing he was participating in the Yatra from Assam and Manipur.

claims.claim: Rahul Gandhi was sitting inside the bus and the person looking out from the window at the people was not him — he was using a body double
claims.notes: Alt News tracked down Rakesh Kushwaha's Facebook profile confirming he was a Congress worker participating in the Yatra from Assam and Manipur. IYC Assam president also confirmed the bus in the original tweet was not Gandhi's bus but a general Yatra bus.

claims.claim: In 1951 the Muslim population in Assam was 14 per cent; today it has increased to 40 per cent. I have lost many districts
claims.impact: Made in Jharkhand as BJP co-in-charge ahead of Jharkhand elections to inflame demographic fears.
claims.notes: Assam CM falsely stated Muslim population in Assam was 14% in 1951. Indian government's own 1951 Census shows it was 22.60%. Alt News confirmed via official Census India website. Both the starting figure and the implied trajectory are false. LEGAL REVIEW NEEDED — communal-violence-attribution context.

claims.claim: The Muslim population of Assam is growing at 30 percent every ten years and is projected to become the majority by 2051
claims.impact: Made in Jharkhand ahead of Jharkhand elections to inflame Hindu voters in the region over demographic fears.
claims.notes: Sarma projected Muslim majority by 2051 based on a false 14%-to-40% growth claim. Census data contradicts the 1951 baseline figure (it was 22.60% not 14%) so the projected trend is also mathematically false. LEGAL REVIEW NEEDED — communal-violence-attribution context.

claims.claim: There is no extradition treaty between India and Bangladesh which is why we use the push-back method to remove Bangladeshi infiltrators
claims.notes: Assam CM made this false legal claim in an ABP News interview justifying 'push-back' operations. The Quint WebQoof found India and Bangladesh signed an extradition treaty on 28 January 2013 amended in 2016 to facilitate expeditious extradition.

claims.claim: Under my government the unemployment rate in Uttar Pradesh has come down from a double-digit figure to 2.9 per cent
claims.notes: Yogi Adityanath at the UP Investors Summit 3 June 2022 claimed unemployment fell from 18% to 2.9%. CMIE data shows when he took charge in 2017 the rate was already 2.4%; it peaked at 21.5% in April 2020. The 18% baseline was fabricated and the claimed 2.9% was not the lowest in his tenure.

claims.claim: No incident of rioting has taken place under his tenure as UP Chief Minister
claims.notes: Yogi Adityanath tweeted on 3 January 2019 that no riots occurred during his tenure. MHA data tabled in Parliament showed 44 killed and 540 injured in UP communal violence in 2017 alone. Multiple communal incidents occurred in Saharanpur (2017) Kasganj (2018) and Bulandshahr (2018).

claims.claim: We have put a check on organised crime and strengthened the rule of law — apart from incidents of family feud and personal animosity people across UP find themselves safe
claims.notes: Second tweet in same thread as the 'no riots' claim. FactChecker.in showed 2018 was the decade's worst year for hate crime with 93 attacks; UP had the highest share. MHA data showed UP topped communal violence stats for 2017. The claim of general safety is contradicted by government's own data.

claims.claim: In the last four years all occasions and festivals in UP were celebrated in a peaceful manner and there have been no riots in the state
claims.notes: Yogi Adityanath made this claim at the UP four-year governance anniversary press conference. NCRB 2019 data showed 5714 cases of rioting in UP making it third nationally. The claim was also re-debunked when he repeated it in April 2023.

claims.claim: According to the NCRB Report of 2019 the conviction percentage in UP for crimes against women is 55.2 per cent which is the highest in the country
claims.notes: Made in context of the Hathras gang-rape case. BOOM found UP's 55.2% conviction rate is the fifth highest nationally not the highest. The claim of being 'highest in the country' is misleading per NCRB data.

claims.claim: Today you can see that the terrorists which were fed Biryani by Congress are now fed bullets by us
claims.impact: Made at Rajasthan election rally to attack Congress.
claims.notes: Yogi Adityanath at a Makrana rally repeated the myth that the Congress government fed biryani to 26/11 terrorist Ajmal Kasab. Alt News confirmed that public prosecutor Ujjwal Nikam stated in 2015 that he himself fabricated the biryani story to counter emotions during the trial; Kasab was never served biryani.

claims.claim: Former Chief Election Commissioner TN Seshan has passed away (condolence tweet based on fake death news)
claims.notes: Union Minister Smriti Irani tweeted condolences based on a fake WhatsApp death hoax circulating for days. BOOM Live had already confirmed with Seshan's family that he was alive before Irani tweeted. The tweet was later deleted. Ironic as Irani was then working on defining anti-fake-news rules.

claims.claim: NASA satellite images show that India has achieved total rural electrification under the BJP government with villages lighting up across the country
claims.notes: Then-Power Minister Goyal shared NASA night-light maps as evidence of rural electrification. Alt News found NASA explicitly states these maps show city lights not village electrification; only 2% of villages were newly electrified in the period claimed. Goyal deleted the tweet.

claims.claim: The 800 MW supercritical thermal unit at the Yeramarus Thermal Power Station in Karnataka was built by NTPC under the BJP government
claims.notes: Then-Power Minister Goyal congratulated NTPC for the Yeramarus unit. In reality the project was a joint venture of KPCL and BHEL under Karnataka state government (RPCL) with no involvement from NTPC. Goyal deleted the tweet after the error was pointed out.

claims.claim: More than 10000 villages have been newly electrified by the BJP government. Nagla Fatela village which could not get power for 70 years has been electrified for the first time
claims.notes: Then-Power Minister Goyal tweeted about Nagla Fatela's electrification. Media subsequently confirmed the village still had no electricity. PM Modi deleted his tweet on the same; Goyal chose not to delete his misleading tweet and it remained up. Alt News also found only 2% of villages were newly electrified in the claimed period.

claims.claim: AR Rahman stated he is proud of eating beef (fake celebrity quote viral on social media)
claims.notes: Then-Defence Minister Nirmala Sitharaman retweeted S Gurumurthy's viral fake quote attributed to AR Rahman about beef. When informed the quote was fabricated she apologised and deleted the retweet. Alt News noted this happened within 24 hours of Sambit Patra's own fake-news tweet.

claims.claim: Rahul Gandhi is openly talking about snatching away the rights and property of Hindus — this is a very frightening mentality
claims.impact: Made during 2024 Lok Sabha election campaign.
claims.notes: BJP MP and Union Minister Giriraj Singh (@girirajsinghbjp) shared a selectively-clipped video of Gandhi's Congress manifesto launch speech. Alt News found Gandhi's full speech mentioned Backward Classes Dalits Tribals poor general caste citizens AND minorities; only the 'minorities' segment was retained in the viral clip.

claims.claim: These photos of Nehru with women show his political alliance with the British and demonstrate his character
claims.notes: Union Textile Minister Giriraj Singh displayed four posters of Nehru outside Parliament claiming they showed his British alliance. Alt News identified three photos as Nehru with his sister Vijaya Lakshmi Pandit and niece; one with a diplomat's wife; one image appears AI-generated. None support the claimed narrative.

claims.claim: The Gujarat Waqf Board claimed ownership over two islands of Beyt Dwarka — an important Hindu religious site associated with Lord Krishna
claims.notes: BJP MP Anurag Thakur made this false claim on the floor of the Lok Sabha during the debate on the Waqf (Amendment) Bill. Alt News found the Gujarat Waqf Board chairman had in 2021 explicitly denied any claim over Beyt Dwarka; the confusion arose from a petition about Shiyal Beyt in Amreli district which has no connection to Beyt Dwarka. Ironically the claim had already been refuted by a BJP MP in 2021.

claims.claim: Anti-CAA protesters in Lucknow's Clock Tower rally were chanting 'Pakistan Zindabad' slogans
claims.notes: Protesters were saying 'Kashif saab zindabad' referring to Lucknow AIMIM chief Kashif Ahmad. Alt News confirmed with AIMIM UP president. LEGAL REVIEW NEEDED (communal incitement potential).

claims.claim: AMU students were chanting 'Hinduon ki kabr khudegi' (grave of Hindus will be dug) — framed as anti-Hindu slogans
claims.notes: Students were shouting slogans against Hindutva, Savarkar, BJP and Brahminism — not against Hindus. Malviya substituted 'Hindutva' with 'Hindus' to give a communal framing. LEGAL REVIEW NEEDED.

claims.claim: Journalist Arfa Khanum's speech at AMU was promoting Islamic supremacy and 'Ghazwa-e-Hind'; 'The Islamists want CAA protests to be inclusive only till you accept their supremacist slogans'
claims.notes: Clipped speech. Khanum was urging protesters NOT to use religious slogans to maintain the secular character of the movement — the opposite of what Malviya claimed. LEGAL REVIEW NEEDED.

claims.claim: Shaheen Bagh protest is sponsored by Congress; women are being paid Rs 500 each to protest against CAA-NRC — shared 'sting' video as evidence
claims.impact: Amplified by Times Now and Republic TV which broadcast the video.
claims.notes: Joint Alt News–Newslaundry investigation found the 'sting' video was shot in a phone shop in Pul Prahladpur 8 km from Shaheen Bagh; the claims in it were hearsay and unverified. The shop owner later confirmed it was shot there and the content was unreliable.

claims.claim: Police 'didn't even touch the farmer' during Singhu border lathi-charge — shared cropped 3-second 'Propaganda vs Reality' video to rebut Rahul Gandhi's photograph
claims.notes: Twitter labelled the tweet 'Manipulated Media' on 2 December 2020 — first-ever such action on an Indian political figure. BOOM Live interviewed farmer Sukhdev Singh who showed bruises from the lathi-charge. Malviya's clip omitted a second officer swinging his baton.

claims.claim: Propaganda vs Reality': Police baton did not touch the farmer at Singhu border — cropped video shared to discredit farmers' protest
claims.notes: WebQoof found a longer version showing police baton almost touching farmer's leg. PTI photographer Ravi Choudhary confirmed police were lathi-charging when old farmer was hit. Tweet received over 300,000 views before Twitter label was applied.

claims.claim: Former PM Manmohan Singh praised BJP state governments in Madhya Pradesh and Chhattisgarh, saying they were 'very good' — contradicting Rahul Gandhi
claims.notes: Clipped video. Singh's full words were: 'My relationships with the government of Madhya Pradesh, the government of Chhattisgarh were very good. We never discriminated against BJP-ruled states.' The clip removed the 'never discriminated' context entirely.

claims.claim: Rahul Gandhi signed the Somnath temple register as a 'non-Hindu'; Gandhis lying about their faith
claims.notes: Handwriting analysis showed the entry in the temple register did not match Gandhi's publicly available handwriting. The claim was used to question Gandhi's Hindu identity ahead of Gujarat elections.

claims.claim: Rahul Gandhi claimed he would install a machine that turns potatoes into gold — shared as a literal boast by Gandhi
claims.notes: Clipped video. In full speech Gandhi explicitly attributed the potato-gold machine quote to PM Modi: 'These are not my words, they are Narendra Modi's words.' Malviya deleted the tweet after fact-check. (translated from Hindi)

claims.claim: Rahul Gandhi visited Gurmeet Ram Rahim's Dera Sacha Sauda in January 2017 to seek support for Punjab elections
claims.notes: Gandhi visited Dera Sach Khand Ballan (a Dalit Ravidassia community shrine) in Jalandhar, not Dera Sacha Sauda. The heads of the two deras are different people. Malviya deleted the tweet after it was debunked.

claims.claim: A man was lynched at Arvind Kejriwal's roadshow — shared video claiming CM was a 'mute spectator' while 'AAP workers lynch a man'
claims.notes: The man was not lynched. Other clips showed Kejriwal was slapped first, and then his supporters beat the assailant. Malviya's video omitted the attack on Kejriwal that preceded the crowd retaliation.

claims.claim: Quoted academic Siva Vaidyanathan (Virginia University) to suggest he was praising BJP for mastering social media — blacked out critical part of quote
claims.notes: Malviya blacked out Vaidyanathan's concerns about WhatsApp misinformation. The full quote showed the professor was worried about the menace of forward cascades, not praising BJP's use of social media.

claims.claim: AIMIM with 2.7% vote share won 7 seats while BJP with 7% vote share won only 1 seat in Telangana — implied BJP was cheated
claims.notes: Numbers were correct but claim was deceptive: AIMIM contested only 8 of 119 seats (strike rate 87.5%) while BJP contested 118 of 119 seats (strike rate 0.85%). Direct comparison of vote share vs seats was misleading without this context.

claims.claim: PM Modi is the first 'head of state' to visit Kumbh Mela in all these years
claims.notes: PM is head of government, not head of state (that is the President). First head of state to visit Kumbh was President Rajendra Prasad. Nehru visited Kumbh in 1954. Both foundational factual claims were wrong.

claims.claim: Nobel economist Richard Thaler praised PM Modi's demonetisation decision
claims.notes: Cherry-picked one tweet out of a conversation. Thaler's direct response when asked was: 'The concept was good but the rollout was deeply flawed and the introduction of the Rs 2000 note makes the motivation puzzling.' Malviya continued using Thaler's name even after the Alt News article.

claims.claim: Yogendra Yadav bragged about his Muslim name to a largely Muslim audience in Mewat to seek votes — shared edited clip as evidence of 'Janus face'
claims.notes: Clip edited out Yadav's key line: 'Na Hindu banega na Musalman banega' (will be neither Hindu nor Muslim). The event was a protest meet after a lynching by a Hindu mob — not an election rally. Yadav's message was to respond to hate with love.

claims.claim: Shared collage of Nehru with women to insinuate Nehru was sexually immoral — linked to Hardik Patel sex CD controversy
claims.notes: Most women in the photographs were Nehru's sister, niece, or world figures like Jacqueline Kennedy. Malviya deleted the tweet but it continued circulating. No retraction or apology was issued.

claims.claim: Alleged first-person account that TMC hooligans orchestrated the vandalisation of Vidyasagar College and Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar's bust during Amit Shah's rally
claims.notes: Alt News investigative report analysed visuals and spoke with faculty and students. Found the post shared by Malviya was misleading. Same text circulated by many users in first-person suggesting coordinated amplification.

claims.claim: Clipped video of Rahul Gandhi's press conference shared out of context implying he was making a 'puerile' and 'dangerous' statement about India's infrastructure
claims.notes: Malviya took a small clip from Gandhi's press briefing out of context. Full video on Gandhi's YouTube channel showed the complete context. Alt News noted this was not the first time Malviya was found spreading misleading information.

claims.claim: Rahul Gandhi was performing aarti in the wrong direction during Bharat Jodo Yatra; also claimed 'earth rotates clockwise'
claims.notes: Gandhi was performing aarti correctly (clockwise). Malviya's additional claim that Earth rotates clockwise is also scientifically false — Earth rotates anticlockwise from west to east, taught in primary schools.

claims.claim: Clipped video of Rahul Gandhi to claim he said he would 'end' (rok denge) the Bharat Jodo Yatra
claims.notes: Gandhi said 'rakh denge' (put it back) referring to his T-shirt. BJP changed the word to 'rok denge' (end) in tweets. Full video context showed he was referring to when he would resume wearing a jacket — not stopping the march.

claims.claim: Rahul Gandhi refused to accept an idol of Bhagwan Vitthal on stage — 'insult to Warkaris and millions of Hindu devotees; Rahul Gandhi suffers from Hindumisia'
claims.impact: Claim reached 370
claims.notes: 600 views before debunk. Used during Lok Sabha election campaign. Extended video (0:18 mark) clearly shows Gandhi accepting the Vitthala idol at Nashik. Malviya's clip ended just before this moment. NCP's Sharad Pawar and other MVA leaders were present. LEGAL REVIEW NEEDED (Hindu religious sentiment framing).

claims.claim: Rahul Gandhi asked India's flag to be taken down — 'straight out of George Soros's rule book'
claims.notes: Gandhi asked for a stick to use as a prop to demonstrate BJP's alleged misuse of central agencies; he asked someone to remove the Congress flag from the stick. Malviya omitted this context entirely.

claims.claim: Video of BJP candidate Dinesh Lal Yadav (Nirahua) commenting on Modi and Yogi being childless to stop unemployment is a 'deepfake' made by Congress with AI
claims.impact: Made during 2024 Lok Sabha elections. Complaint filed with Election Commission based on Malviya's false deepfake claim.
claims.notes: BOOM used deepfake detection tools and retrieved original video from reporter Santosh Kushwaha who confirmed it was real, shot on 13 April 2024 in Azamgarh. The Quint WebQoof corroborated. Malviya's false deepfake accusation led to BJP filing EC complaint against Congress.

claims.claim: Video of BJP's Nirahua making remarks about Modi and Yogi being childless is a deepfake created by Congress
claims.impact: 2024 Lok Sabha election campaign.
claims.notes: WebQoof AI detection found no evidence of deepfake. Journalist who shot video confirmed its authenticity. Video was real; editing only involved sequence reordering — reporter confirmed full original.

claims.claim: Yogendra Yadav openly admitted that Rahul Gandhi's 'vote theft' (vote chori) campaign has no proof and is being peddled to malign PM Modi and project Rahul Gandhi
claims.notes: Gross misinterpretation. Yadav actually said Gandhi's allegations were shaping public perception and making people question BJP and the ECI's credentials. Alt News called it 'baseless and presented out of context.' BJP4India official account shared the same clip simultaneously.

claims.claim: Sonam Wangchuk urged protesters to come masked and hooded — implied incitement to violence during Ladakh protests
claims.impact: Shared during Wangchuk's September 2025 arrest by Leh police.
claims.notes: Video dated from September 8 — before Wangchuk's hunger strike announcement. In it he was inviting followers to the Sarva Dharm Prarthana Sabha and urging masks to prevent COVID spread in crowds and caps to shield from sun. LEGAL REVIEW NEEDED (context of activist's arrest).

claims.claim: Sonam Wangchuk said Ladakh should bring about change 'like Nepal, Bangladesh and Sri Lanka' — implied call for violent uprising
claims.impact: Shared after Wangchuk arrest.
claims.notes: Full 34-minute video showed Wangchuk explicitly urging peaceful change: 'unlike others, Ladakh does not need to resort to violence, stone-pelting, or chaos.' Malviya's clip removed the preceding and following sentences that reversed the meaning. LEGAL REVIEW NEEDED.

claims.claim: Rahul Gandhi claimed 'a single woman voted 223 times across two booths in Haryana' — mocked as 'imagination gone global'
claims.notes: Gandhi said the same image appeared against 223 different voter IDs — alleging proxy voters. He never claimed one person voted 223 times. Malviya's mockery deliberately misrepresented the allegation.

claims.claim: Multiple false linguistic claims defending Delhi Police using 'Bangladeshi language' phrase: (1) Sylheti dialect incomprehensible to Indian Bengalis; (2) there is no unified language called Bengali; (3) Bengali denotes ethnicity not linguistic uniformity; (4) 'Bangladeshi language' is a valid shorthand for linguistic markers of illegal immigrants
claims.notes: Alt News fact-checked all four claims and found them false from the standpoint of linguistics. Sylheti is comprehensible to Bengali speakers; Bengali is a recognised unified language per Indian Constitution's Eighth Schedule; no such entity as 'Bangladeshi language' exists — even Bangladesh's own Constitution calls its language Bangla/Bengali.

claims.claim: West Bengal CM Mamata Banerjee 'casually mocked the demolition of a temple' — shared 8-second clip as evidence
claims.impact: Shared three weeks before 2026 West Bengal assembly elections.
claims.notes: Banerjee's full speech showed she said: 'Demolishing temples is not right. I do not endorse it.' She was pointing to BJP's alleged double standards re: Kashi Viswanath corridor demolitions. Clip removed context entirely. LEGAL REVIEW NEEDED (election eve communal framing).

claims.claim: TMC candidate Byron Biswas admitted defeat and named other constituencies where TMC would lose — 27-second cropped clip shared
claims.impact: During 2026 West Bengal elections.
claims.notes: Longer 5.28-minute video showed Biswas alleging foul play after CCTV malfunction in Jangipur EVM strongroom. He was responding to a reporter's question about what he fears. BOOM Live corroborated the finding. Biswas threatened legal action against Malviya.

claims.claim: Propaganda vs Reality': police baton did not touch elderly Sikh farmer at Singhu border — shared cropped video to rebut Rahul Gandhi
claims.notes: BOOM Live identified farmer Sukhdev Singh from Kapurthala who showed bruises and said 'I am right here if they want to come and see my injuries.' Twitter labelled tweet 'Manipulated Media' on Dec 2 2020 — first use of label on Indian political figure. Notes: BOOM article at boomlive.in confirmed via Indian Express report on same date. Twitter labelled tweet Manipulated Media on 2 Dec 2020 citing BOOM Live analysis. First Twitter manipulated media label on Indian political figure. Farmer Sukhdev Singh interviewed by BOOM showed bruises.

claims.claim: Rahul Gandhi's advisor Yogendra Yadav himself admitted — the entire 'vote theft' campaign is being run without any evidence; a conspiracy to tarnish PM Modi's image
claims.notes: BJP4India official account and Amit Malviya shared same clipped video simultaneously. Yadav's actual message was the opposite: he said Gandhi's allegations were shaping public perception and making voters question BJP and the ECI. Alt News found the claim 'baseless and presented out of context.'

claims.claim: Campaign video showing dilapidated roads in Delhi to attack AAP government — roads were actually in BJP-governed Faridabad, Haryana
claims.impact: Delhi Assembly elections campaign January 2025.
claims.notes: BJP released campaign video showing various deteriorating roads criticising AAP Delhi government. WebQoof found the visuals were from Faridabad — governed by BJP in Haryana, not Delhi.

claims.claim: Rahul Gandhi was performing aarti in the wrong direction during Bharat Jodo Yatra Narmada aarti
claims.notes: Amplified by BJP accounts. Gandhi was in fact performing aarti correctly (clockwise as per Hindu tradition). Malviya's additional scientific error — claiming Earth rotates clockwise — was also corrected.

claims.claim: Video of a man saying 'I am a Muslim first and then an Indian' is Congress leader Nasimuddin Siddiqui
claims.notes: India Today AFWA found the man was Samajwadi Party leader Maviya Ali — not Siddiqui. Sambit Patra made a similar claim; he later corrected himself. Siddiqui filed a police complaint against those circulating the video and named Tarek Fatah and Sambit Patra. LEGAL REVIEW NEEDED.

claims.claim: Shared a clipped video of journalist Ravish Kumar after Gauri Lankesh murder to make it appear Kumar was deflecting accountability away from nationalists
claims.notes: Full speech showed Kumar was addressing PM Modi directly, urging him to ask his party workers that 'nationalist Hindutva is not nationalist'. The clip cut out the context showing 'woh' referred to Modi. Shared days after Gauri Lankesh murder. LEGAL REVIEW NEEDED.

claims.claim: Official BJP West Bengal Facebook page used photo of live journalist Abhro Banerjee identifying him as 'Manik Moitra' — killed by TMC workers in post-poll violence
claims.impact: During post-2021 West Bengal election violence coverage.
claims.notes: Banerjee tweeted 'BJP IT Cell is now claiming I am Manik Moitra and died in Sitalkuchi. Please don't believe these fake posts — I repeat: I am (still) alive.' BJP issued statement hours later claiming it was 'erroneously included.' Video was removed after exposure. LEGAL REVIEW NEEDED.

claims.claim: Mamata Banerjee recited only an Islamic verse — used to accuse her of 'appeasement politics'; video is from recent BJP West Bengal Twitter campaign
claims.impact: During 2021 West Bengal assembly elections.
claims.notes: Full longer video (from 2018) showed Banerjee reciting verses from multiple religions, not just Islam. BJP4Bengal account saw over 2,000 retweets. WebQoof tied this to a coordinated BJP hashtag campaign with text packets distributed via Google Drive (since deleted).

claims.claim: Rahul Gandhi called the entire Hindu community violent in Parliament speech — 'Those who call themselves Hindus engage in violence, hatred and lies 24 hours a day'
claims.notes: Full Parliament TV footage showed Gandhi immediately clarified: 'No, No, No, I'm saying this about the BJP and about you. Modiji is not the entire Hindu community.' BJP MPs, Union Ministers, and Right-wing influencers all shared the clipped version. LEGAL REVIEW NEEDED.

claims.claim: Claimed PM Modi is 'first head of state to take a dip in Kumbh' — tweeted within hours of Modi's Prayagraj Kumbh dip
claims.impact: Ahead of 2019 Lok Sabha elections.
claims.notes: PM is head of government not head of state (President is). First head of state at Kumbh was President Rajendra Prasad. First PM was Nehru in 1954. Claim wrong on two separate factual grounds.
